first_paragraph: ***Green Lantern*** is a 2011 American [superhero film](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film") based on the [character](/wiki/Green_Lantern "Green Lantern") from [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics"). It was directed by [Martin Campbell](/wiki/Martin_Campbell "Martin Campbell"), from a screenplay by [Greg Berlanti](/wiki/Greg_Berlanti "Greg Berlanti"), [Michael Green](/wiki/Michael_Green_(writer) "Michael Green (writer)"), [Marc Guggenheim](/wiki/Marc_Guggenheim "Marc Guggenheim"), and [Michael Goldenberg](/wiki/Michael_Goldenberg "Michael Goldenberg").[[a]](#cite_note-3) [Ryan Reynolds](/wiki/Ryan_Reynolds "Ryan Reynolds") stars as [Hal Jordan / Green Lantern](/wiki/Hal_Jordan "Hal Jordan"), a test pilot who is selected to become the first human member of the [Green Lantern Corps](/wiki/Green_Lantern_Corps "Green Lantern Corps"), an intergalactic police force. He is given a [ring that grants him superpowers](/wiki/Power_ring_(DC_Comics) "Power ring (DC Comics)") and must confront [Parallax](/wiki/Parallax_(character) "Parallax (character)"), a being who threatens to upset the balance of power in the universe. The film also stars [Blake Lively](/wiki/Blake_Lively "Blake Lively"), [Peter Sarsgaard](/wiki/Peter_Sarsgaard "Peter Sarsgaard"), [Mark Strong](/wiki/Mark_Strong "Mark Strong"), [Angela Bassett](/wiki/Angela_Bassett "Angela Bassett"), and [Tim Robbins](/wiki/Tim_Robbins "Tim Robbins").

***Green Lantern*** is a 2011 American [superhero film](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film") based on the [character](/wiki/Green_Lantern "Green Lantern") from [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics"). It was directed by [Martin Campbell](/wiki/Martin_Campbell "Martin Campbell"), from a screenplay by [Greg Berlanti](/wiki/Greg_Berlanti "Greg Berlanti"), [Michael Green](/wiki/Michael_Green_(writer) "Michael Green (writer)"), [Marc Guggenheim](/wiki/Marc_Guggenheim "Marc Guggenheim"), and [Michael Goldenberg](/wiki/Michael_Goldenberg "Michael Goldenberg").[[a]](#cite_note-3) [Ryan Reynolds](/wiki/Ryan_Reynolds "Ryan Reynolds") stars as [Hal Jordan / Green Lantern](/wiki/Hal_Jordan "Hal Jordan"), a test pilot who is selected to become the first human member of the [Green Lantern Corps](/wiki/Green_Lantern_Corps "Green Lantern Corps"), an intergalactic police force. He is given a [ring that grants him superpowers](/wiki/Power_ring_(DC_Comics) "Power ring (DC Comics)") and must confront [Parallax](/wiki/Parallax_(character) "Parallax (character)"), a being who threatens to upset the balance of power in the universe. The film also stars [Blake Lively](/wiki/Blake_Lively "Blake Lively"), [Peter Sarsgaard](/wiki/Peter_Sarsgaard "Peter Sarsgaard"), [Mark Strong](/wiki/Mark_Strong "Mark Strong"), [Angela Bassett](/wiki/Angela_Bassett "Angela Bassett"), and [Tim Robbins](/wiki/Tim_Robbins "Tim Robbins").

The film first entered [development](/wiki/Development_(film) "Development (film)") in 1997; progress remained stalled until Berlanti was hired to write and direct in October 2007. [Martin Campbell](/wiki/Martin_Campbell "Martin Campbell") was brought on board in February 2009 after Berlanti was forced to vacate the director's position. Most of the live-action actors were cast between July 2009 and February 2010, and filming took place from March to August 2010 in [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"). The film was converted to [3D](/wiki/3-D_film "3-D film") during its post-production stage. This was the first DC film since [*Catwoman*](/wiki/Catwoman_(film) "Catwoman (film)") (2004) not to be involved with [Legendary Pictures](/wiki/Legendary_Pictures "Legendary Pictures").

*Green Lantern* was released in the United States on June 17, 2011, by [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures"). The film received negative reviews from critics, with criticism for the direction, script, tone, visual effects, humor, and unfaithfulness to the source material, though Reynolds' performance was praised. It grossed only $237 million against a $200 million budget. Intended to start a media franchise and shared universe based on DC characters, Warner Bros. scrapped the plans due to the film's critical and commercial disappointment; instead using *[Man of Steel](/wiki/Man_of_Steel_(film) "Man of Steel (film)")* (2013) as the first film of the [DC Extended Universe](/wiki/DC_Extended_Universe "DC Extended Universe") two years later.[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Billions of years ago, the [Guardians of the Universe](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Universe "Guardians of the Universe") used the green essence of [willpower](/wiki/Will_(philosophy) "Will (philosophy)") to create an intergalactic police force called the [Green Lantern Corps](/wiki/Green_Lantern_Corps "Green Lantern Corps"). They divided the universe into 3,600 sectors, with one assigned Green Lantern per sector.

[Abin Sur](/wiki/Abin_Sur "Abin Sur") of Sector 2814 defeats the malevolent being [Parallax](/wiki/Parallax_(character) "Parallax (character)"), and imprisons him in the Lost Sector on the desolate planet Ryut. Years later, Parallax escapes from prison after becoming strengthened by an encounter with crash survivors who had accidentally fallen into the dugout where he was imprisoned. Parallax pursues and mortally wounds Abin Sur. Abin Sur escapes, and crash-lands on [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), commanding his [power ring](/wiki/Power_ring_(DC_Comics) "Power ring (DC Comics)") to find a worthy successor.

[Hal Jordan](/wiki/Hal_Jordan "Hal Jordan"), a cocky test pilot working at Ferris Aircraft, is chosen by the ring, and transported to the crash site. Abin Sur provides Hal with his ring, telling him to place it in the lantern and speak [the oath](/wiki/Green_Lantern#Green_Lantern_Oath "Green Lantern"), before succumbing from his wounds. Hal takes the lantern, recites the oath, and appoints himself as a Green Lantern. The ring teleports Hal across [outer space](/wiki/Outer_space "Outer space") to Green Lantern’s homeworld [Oa](/wiki/Oa_(comics) "Oa (comics)"), where he meets veteran Corps members: [Tomar-Re](/wiki/Tomar-Re "Tomar-Re"), [Kilowog](/wiki/Kilowog "Kilowog"), and leader [Sinestro](/wiki/Sinestro "Sinestro"). During his extreme training sessions, Sinestro believes Hal is unable to harness the power of the ring due to his lack of will. Disheartened by Sinestro's doubts, Hal quits and returns to Earth, keeping the power ring.

Scientist [Hector Hammond](/wiki/Hector_Hammond "Hector Hammond") is summoned by his father, Senator Robert Hammond, to a [secret government facility](/wiki/Department_of_Extranormal_Operations "Department of Extranormal Operations") to perform an autopsy on Abin Sur's body under supervision of [Amanda Waller](/wiki/Amanda_Waller "Amanda Waller"). A piece of Parallax's DNA inside the corpse enters Hammond, giving him psychic powers at the cost of his health and sanity. Hector develops a personal resentment towards his father, and plans to kill him by [telekinetically](/wiki/Psychokinesis "Psychokinesis") sabotaging his helicopter at a massive party. Hal saves the senator and party guests, including his childhood sweetheart [Carol Ferris](/wiki/Carol_Ferris "Carol Ferris"). Hector rampages the government facility, and uses his telekinesis to incinerate his surviving father. Hal [telepathically](/wiki/Telepathy "Telepathy") connects with Hector, and realizes Parallax approaching Earth to devour fear from its inhabitants.

Back in Oa, the Guardians narrate to Sinestro that one of their fellow guardians became Parallax after a backfired attempt to control the forbidden yellow essence of fear. Arguing that the only way to fight fear is with fear itself, Sinestro proposes to forge a ring of the same yellow energy, preparing to concede Earth's destruction to Parallax in order to protect Oa. Hal reasons to the Guardians, and Sinestro that fear will turn the users evil if its power is used, but they reject his pleas. He returns to Earth to try to defeat Parallax on his own.

Hal saves Carol from Hammond after a brief showdown. Parallax arrives, consumes Hammond's entire life force, and wreaks havoc on Coast City. After a fierce ground battle, Hal lures Parallax away from Earth to chase him across the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System"). Hal successfully traps Parallax in the [gravitational pull](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") of the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") where it is destroyed. Hal loses consciousness, but is saved by Sinestro, Kilowog, and Tomar-Re. After returning to Oa, the entire Green Lantern Corps congratulate Hal for his courage, and bravery in defeating Parallax. Sinestro tells him that he now bears the responsibility of protecting his sector as a Green Lantern. Hal and Carol spend one last time together, before Hal leaves Earth.

In a [mid-credits scene](/wiki/Post-credits_scene "Post-credits scene"), Sinestro secretly takes the yellow ring, causing his green suit and eyes to turn yellow, foreshadowing a [rebellion](/wiki/Sinestro_Corps "Sinestro Corps") against the Corps.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

In early 1997, [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures") approached [cult filmmaker](/wiki/Cult_film "Cult film") and comic book writer [Kevin Smith](/wiki/Kevin_Smith "Kevin Smith"), who had then just finished writing *[Superman Lives](/wiki/Superman_in_film#Superman_Lives "Superman in film")*, to script a *Green Lantern* film. Smith turned down the offer, believing there were more suitable candidates to make a *Green Lantern* film.[[38]](#cite_note-39) At one point, [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino") was offered the chance to write and direct.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Warner Bros. also considered the property as an action comedy; by 2004, [Robert Smigel](/wiki/Robert_Smigel "Robert Smigel") had completed a script which was set to star [Jack Black](/wiki/Jack_Black "Jack Black") in the lead role, but the studio dropped the comedy idea following poor fan reaction from the Internet and chose to focus on a serious Green Lantern film instead.[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44) [David S. Goyer](/wiki/David_S._Goyer "David S. Goyer") was offered the chance to write and direct either a Green Lantern or [The Flash](/wiki/The_Flash "The Flash") film after Warner Bros. was impressed with his screenplay for *[Batman Begins](/wiki/Batman_Begins "Batman Begins")*, but he opted to direct the latter.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

Actor-writer [Corey Reynolds](/wiki/Corey_Reynolds "Corey Reynolds"), a comic book fan of the [John Stewart](/wiki/John_Stewart_(character) "John Stewart (character)") character, [pitched](/wiki/Pitch_(filmmaking) "Pitch (filmmaking)") to Warner Bros. an idea for a trilogy, with him starring and performing screenwriting duties.[[45]](#cite_note-Melrose-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47) He finished the script for *Green Lantern: Birth of a Hero* in June 2007, receiving positive feedback from Warner Bros. Reynolds hoped for a potential 2010 release date.[[45]](#cite_note-Melrose-46) However, for unknown reasons, the studio abandoned Reynolds' concept. In October the same year, [Greg Berlanti](/wiki/Greg_Berlanti "Greg Berlanti") signed to direct the film and cowrite it with comic book writers [Michael Green](/wiki/Michael_Green_(writer) "Michael Green (writer)") and [Marc Guggenheim](/wiki/Marc_Guggenheim "Marc Guggenheim").[[47]](#cite_note-48) A draft of the trio's 2008 script, leaked on the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), revealed a story that included the hero's origin and included the characters [Carol Ferris](/wiki/Carol_Ferris "Carol Ferris"), [Kilowog](/wiki/Kilowog "Kilowog"), [Sinestro](/wiki/Sinestro "Sinestro"), and [Guy Gardner](/wiki/Guy_Gardner_(character) "Guy Gardner (character)") in a [cameo appearance](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance"), and appeared "to set up Hector Hammond as Hal Jordan's ... first major nemesis...."[[48]](#cite_note-49) Earlier drafts of the script also included a [Superman](/wiki/Superman "Superman") cameo appearance, which was later scrapped.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Shortly afterward, Guggenheim said that the script would contain characterizations inspired by [Dennis O'Neil](/wiki/Dennis_O%27Neil "Dennis O'Neil") and [Neal Adams](/wiki/Neal_Adams "Neal Adams")' run on *[Green Lantern](/wiki/Green_Lantern_(comic_book) "Green Lantern (comic book)")* in the 1970s, and [Dave Gibbons](/wiki/Dave_Gibbons "Dave Gibbons")' work in the early 1980s. He added that he and his co-writers also looked to the 2000s [Geoff Johns](/wiki/Geoff_Johns "Geoff Johns") stories: "It's been interesting because we finished a draft just before Johns' "[Secret Origin](/wiki/Green_Lantern:_Secret_Origin "Green Lantern: Secret Origin")" story arc started up. So I've been reading "Secret Origin" with a real interest in seeing 'OK, how did Geoff solve this problem?' There are certain elements just for anyone trying to retell Hal's origin for a modern-day audience to address and grapple with. For example, why the hell was [Abin Sur](/wiki/Abin_Sur "Abin Sur") flying in a spaceship when he's a Green Lantern? You don't ask that question back in the [Silver Age](/wiki/Silver_Age_of_Comic_Books "Silver Age of Comic Books"), but when you're writing in the [Modern Age](/wiki/Modern_Age_of_Comic_Books "Modern Age of Comic Books"), you have to answer these things".[[50]](#cite_note-51)

### Pre-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pre-production")]

By December 2008, the writers had written three drafts of the screenplay and Warner Bros. was preparing for [pre-production](/wiki/Pre-production "Pre-production").[[51]](#cite_note-52) By February 2009 Berlanti was no longer attached to the project and [Martin Campbell](/wiki/Martin_Campbell "Martin Campbell") entered negotiations to direct.[[52]](#cite_note-53) The release date was set as December 2010, before being moved to June 17, 2011.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Speaking about the experience in 2016, Berlanti confirmed that he was fired as both director and writer, stating that he had nothing to do with the finished product.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

[Bradley Cooper](/wiki/Bradley_Cooper "Bradley Cooper"),[[55]](#cite_note-collider-56) [Ryan Reynolds](/wiki/Ryan_Reynolds "Ryan Reynolds"),[[55]](#cite_note-collider-56) [Justin Timberlake](/wiki/Justin_Timberlake "Justin Timberlake"),[[55]](#cite_note-collider-56) and [Jared Leto](/wiki/Jared_Leto "Jared Leto")[[5]](#cite_note-reynolds-6) were the producers' top choices for the starring role in July 2009. On July 10, Reynolds was cast as Hal Jordan/Green Lantern.[[5]](#cite_note-reynolds-6) Reynolds took the role after [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox") had no intention to make a [Deadpool](/wiki/Deadpool "Deadpool") film.[[56]](#cite_note-57) A website reported on January 7, 2010, that a crew-member had written on her blog that the film was [greenlit](/wiki/Greenlit "Greenlit") the day before and that filming would begin in 10 weeks.[[57]](#cite_note-58) Also in January, [Blake Lively](/wiki/Blake_Lively "Blake Lively") was cast as [Carol Ferris](/wiki/Carol_Ferris "Carol Ferris"),[[17]](#cite_note-lively-18) [Peter Sarsgaard](/wiki/Peter_Sarsgaard "Peter Sarsgaard") as [Hector Hammond](/wiki/Hector_Hammond "Hector Hammond"),[[21]](#cite_note-sarsgaard-22) and [Mark Strong](/wiki/Mark_Strong "Mark Strong") as [Sinestro](/wiki/Sinestro "Sinestro").[[23]](#cite_note-strong-24) In February, [Tim Robbins](/wiki/Tim_Robbins "Tim Robbins") joined the cast as Senator Hammond.[[27]](#cite_note-robbins-28) The following month, [New Zealanders](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") [Temuera Morrison](/wiki/Temuera_Morrison "Temuera Morrison") and [Taika Waititi](/wiki/Taika_Waititi "Taika Waititi") had joined the cast as Abin Sur and Tom Kalmaku, respectively.[[28]](#cite_note-morrison-29)

### Filming

School bus marked with Green Lantern's fictional "[Coast City](/wiki/Coast_City "Coast City")" used in a shot in the [Carrollton section](/wiki/Carrollton,_Louisiana "Carrollton, Louisiana") of [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), July 2010

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Lakefront_Airport_NOLA_May_2010_1.JPG/250px-Lakefront_Airport_NOLA_May_2010_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lakefront_Airport_NOLA_May_2010_1.JPG)

With a production budget of $200 million,[[58]](#cite_note-BOM-59)
[[59]](#cite_note-budget-60) *Green Lantern* was initially scheduled to begin filming in November 2009 at [Fox Studios Australia](/wiki/Fox_Studios_Australia "Fox Studios Australia").[[60]](#cite_note-61) The start date was pushed back to January 2010,[[5]](#cite_note-reynolds-6) but the production moved to [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), where, on March 3, test footage was filmed in [Madisonville](/wiki/Madisonville,_Louisiana "Madisonville, Louisiana") involving stunt cars.[[61]](#cite_note-62) [Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") began on March 15 in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), which doubled for [Coast City](/wiki/Coast_City "Coast City"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), a fictional city in DC Comics.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Nine days after filming began, [Angela Bassett](/wiki/Angela_Bassett "Angela Bassett") joined the cast as [Amanda Waller](/wiki/Amanda_Waller "Amanda Waller"), a government agent who is a staple of the DC Comics universe.[[25]](#cite_note-bassett-26)

The same year in April, [Jon Tenney](/wiki/Jon_Tenney "Jon Tenney") was cast as Hal Jordan's father, test pilot Martin H. Jordan.[[36]](#cite_note-tenney-37) Against Campbell's wishes, the film's production head decided to have the sequence of Martin's death in an air crash intercut with Hal plunging in the plane and witnessing the flashbacks coming to him.[[63]](#cite_note-CampbellScreenRant-64) By June, filming began at [New Orleans Lakefront Airport](/wiki/New_Orleans_Lakefront_Airport "New Orleans Lakefront Airport").[[64]](#cite_note-65) In the same month, [Mike Doyle](/wiki/Mike_Doyle_(actor) "Mike Doyle (actor)") was cast as Jack Jordan, the older brother of Hal Jordan. In July, Ryan Reynolds was injured while shooting scenes for the film, separating his shoulder and in "lots of pain".[[65]](#cite_note-66)

While promoting *[Deadpool](/wiki/Deadpool_(film) "Deadpool (film)")* (in which Reynolds portrays another famous [comic book superhero](/wiki/Deadpool "Deadpool")) in 2016, Reynolds said that filming *Green Lantern* itself had been frustrating: "You really need a visionary behind a movie like that, but it was the classic studio story: 'We have a poster, but we don't have a script or know what we want; let's start shooting!'"[[66]](#cite_note-67) In an exclusive interview with *[Screen Rant](/wiki/Screen_Rant "Screen Rant")* in 2021, Campbell reflected that he should not have directed the film after all and admitted responsibility for the film "not working out". Comparing to how he saw all *[James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond")* films before directing *[Casino Royale](/wiki/Casino_Royale_(2006_film) "Casino Royale (2006 film)")*, Campbell acknowledged that superhero movies were never his "cup of tea".[[63]](#cite_note-CampbellScreenRant-64) In 2025, Campbell also stated that the film would have been better had Reynolds also written the script.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

### Post-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Post-production")]

Principal photography ended on August 6, 2010.[[68]](#cite_note-69) In an interview with *[MTV News](/wiki/MTV_News "MTV News")*, director Martin Campbell was asked about the film's effects-heavy epic scale and commented that it was a daunting process and that the film has 1,300 visual shots.[[69]](#cite_note-70) When asked about the constructs created from the [power rings](/wiki/Power_ring_(DC_Comics) "Power ring (DC Comics)"), Campbell answered: "One of the nice things is, we'll all sit down and say, 'Well, what are we going to do here?' Really, it's as much as your imagination can go to make the constructs".[[70]](#cite_note-71) The studio also confirmed that the film would have a [3D](/wiki/3D_film "3D film") release.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

In January 2011, *Green Lantern* began re-shoots for key scenes at Warner Bros. Studios in Los Angeles, California.[[72]](#cite_note-73) In March, [Geoffrey Rush](/wiki/Geoffrey_Rush "Geoffrey Rush") joined the cast as the voice of the [CGI](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery")-created character, [Tomar-Re](/wiki/Tomar-Re "Tomar-Re").[[30]](#cite_note-rush-31) In April, [Michael Clarke Duncan](/wiki/Michael_Clarke_Duncan "Michael Clarke Duncan") was cast as the voice of [Kilowog](/wiki/Kilowog "Kilowog").[[32]](#cite_note-duncan-33) Also that month, Warner Bros. raised the visual effects budget by $9 million and hired additional visual effects studios to bolster the ranks of the team that had been working overtime to meet the film's release date.[[73]](#cite_note-74) The film's theatrical running time ended up being 114 minutes.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Green Lantern (soundtrack)](/wiki/Green_Lantern_(soundtrack) "Green Lantern (soundtrack)")

The *Green Lantern* score was composed by [James Newton Howard](/wiki/James_Newton_Howard "James Newton Howard"),[[75]](#cite_note-IGNScore-76) who also worked on the other DC Comics-based films *[Batman Begins](/wiki/Batman_Begins "Batman Begins")* and *[The Dark Knight](/wiki/The_Dark_Knight "The Dark Knight")* with [Hans Zimmer](/wiki/Hans_Zimmer "Hans Zimmer"), performed by the [Hollywood Studio Symphony](/wiki/Hollywood_Studio_Symphony "Hollywood Studio Symphony") and conducted by Pete Anthony.[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78) The soundtrack was published by [WaterTower Music](/wiki/WaterTower_Music "WaterTower Music"),[[78]](#cite_note-79) and released in stores on June 14, 2011.[[75]](#cite_note-IGNScore-76)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Release")]

### Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Marketing")]

Marketing and promotional materials for the film totaled $100 million.[[79]](#cite_note-costs-80) The first footage of the film was shown at the 2010 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con") and was widely released online in November with thirty seconds of footage airing the following day on *[Entertainment Tonight](/wiki/Entertainment_Tonight "Entertainment Tonight")*.[[80]](#cite_note-81) The first full theatrical trailer for the film was shown before screenings of *[Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows – Part 1](/wiki/Harry_Potter_and_the_Deathly_Hallows_%E2%80%93_Part_1 "Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows – Part 1")*[[81]](#cite_note-82) and became available online in November.[[82]](#cite_note-83) This initial trailer was met with a poor reception from fans and, as a result, the film's marketing campaign was delayed. Sue Kroll, the studio's worldwide marketing president responded: "Part of the reason the response to the first trailer was lukewarm was that the big-scale sequences weren't ready to show, and we suffered for it. We can't afford to do that again".[[83]](#cite_note-84) In April, Warner Bros. debuted nine minutes of footage at the 2011 [WonderCon](/wiki/WonderCon "WonderCon") in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"). *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* reported that the footage wowed the audience. A four-minute cut of the WonderCon footage was later released online.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

#### Animation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Animation")]

In March 2010, *Comics Continuum* reported that an animated *Green Lantern* film was in the works at [Warner Bros. Animation](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Animation "Warner Bros. Animation"), as a direct-to-video project that was timed for the release of the live-action *Green Lantern* movie in the summer of 2011. The Green Lantern animated project was described as taking a look at the origins of the Green Lantern Corps, including the first ring wielders.[[85]](#cite_note-86) In an interview with [Bruce Timm](/wiki/Bruce_Timm "Bruce Timm"), the producer revealed that a sequel to the [*Green Lantern* animated movie](/wiki/Green_Lantern:_First_Flight "Green Lantern: First Flight") was discussed but canceled because of the picture not achieving the immediate success that they had hoped for, although Timm hoped that the live-action film would renew interest in a sequel.[[86]](#cite_note-87) The animated film entitled *[Green Lantern: Emerald Knights](/wiki/Green_Lantern:_Emerald_Knights "Green Lantern: Emerald Knights")* was officially announced in June instead.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

#### Comics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Comics")]

DC Entertainment began releasing a series of *Green Lantern Movie Prequel* comics the week before the film was released, covering the lives of the characters before the events of the film, written by members of the film's production team. Five comics were made, covering Tomar-Re by Marc Guggenheim, Kilowog by [Peter Tomasi](/wiki/Peter_Tomasi "Peter Tomasi"), Abin Sur by Michael Green, Hal Jordan by Greg Berlanti, and Sinestro by Michael Goldenberg and Geoff Johns. A free excerpt of the Sinestro prequel comic was released online as "Secret Origin of the Green Lantern Corps #1" two days before the release of the film.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

#### Roller coaster

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Roller coaster")]

[Six Flags](/wiki/Six_Flags "Six Flags") debuted two roller coasters named Green Lantern at [Great Adventure](/wiki/Green_Lantern_(Six_Flags_Great_Adventure) "Green Lantern (Six Flags Great Adventure)") and [Magic Mountain](/wiki/Green_Lantern:_First_Flight_(roller_coaster) "Green Lantern: First Flight (roller coaster)") in 2011 to coincide with the film's release.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

#### Video game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Video game")]

[Warner Bros. Interactive](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Interactive_Entertainment "Warner Bros. Interactive Entertainment") produced a tie-in video game, *[Green Lantern: Rise of the Manhunters](/wiki/Green_Lantern:_Rise_of_the_Manhunters "Green Lantern: Rise of the Manhunters")*, for the [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3") and [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360") by [Double Helix Games](/wiki/Double_Helix_Games "Double Helix Games"), with versions for the [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii"), [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS"), and [Nintendo 3DS](/wiki/Nintendo_3DS "Nintendo 3DS") by [Griptonite Games](/wiki/Griptonite_Games "Griptonite Games").[[90]](#cite_note-91)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Home media")]

*Green Lantern* was released on [DVD](/wiki/DVD-Video "DVD-Video") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") on October 14, 2011. The Blu-ray release includes an extended cut, which adds an extra nine minutes of footage to the running time, totaling 123 minutes.[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Box office")]

*Green Lantern* had its [world premiere](/wiki/Premiere "Premiere") at [Grauman's Chinese Theatre](/wiki/Grauman%27s_Chinese_Theatre "Grauman's Chinese Theatre") in [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles"), California on June 15, 2011.[[93]](#cite_note-94) *Green Lantern* opened on Friday, June 17, 2011, in North America,[[94]](#cite_note-95) earning $3.4 million in 1,180 midnight runs.[[95]](#cite_note-96) The film grossed $21.4 million on its opening day. It earned $53.2 million during its opening weekend, taking the number-one spot.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

In its second weekend, *Green Lantern* dropped into third place behind *[Cars 2](/wiki/Cars_2 "Cars 2")* and *[Bad Teacher](/wiki/Bad_Teacher "Bad Teacher")*, experiencing a 66 percent decline, which was the largest second-weekend decline for a superhero film at the time.[[97]](#cite_note-98) *Green Lantern* grossed $116.6 million in the United States and Canada, as well as $120.6 million internationally, bringing its worldwide total to $237.2 million.[[58]](#cite_note-BOM-59) Many industry analysts felt that *Green Lantern* "failed to perform to expectations".[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100) *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* speculated that *Green Lantern* needed to make approximately $500 million to be considered financially solid.[[100]](#cite_note-101) Some publications listed the losses for the studio as high as $75 million.[[101]](#cite_note-InsiderLosses-102)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Critical response")]

On the [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film holds an approval rating of 25% based on 248 reviews. The site's critical consensus reads: "Noisy, overproduced, and thinly written, *Green Lantern* squanders an impressive budget and decades of comics mythology".[[102]](#cite_note-103) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which uses a weighted average, assigned the film a score of 39 out of 100 from 39 critics, indicating "generally unfavorable" reviews.[[103]](#cite_note-104) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "B" on an A+ to F scale.[[104]](#cite_note-105)[[105]](#cite_note-106)

Justin Chang of *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* gave *Green Lantern* a mixed review, stating that "Martin Campbell's visually lavish sci-fi adventure is a highly unstable alloy of the serious, the goofy and the downright derivative".[[106]](#cite_note-107) [Manohla Dargis](/wiki/Manohla_Dargis "Manohla Dargis") of the *[New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* was generally negative to the film, despite having some praise for Ryan Reynolds, Peter Sarsgaard, Mark Strong, and Angela Bassett.[[107]](#cite_note-NYTReview-108) [Christy Lemire](/wiki/Christy_Lemire "Christy Lemire") of the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") called it a "joyless amalgamation of expository dialogue and special effects that aren't especially special".[[108]](#cite_note-109) [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") of the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")* had mixed feelings: "*Green Lantern* does not intend to be plausible. It intends to be a sound-and-light show, assaulting the audience with sensational special effects. If that's what you want, that's what you get".[[109]](#cite_note-110) British newspaper *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* named *Green Lantern* one of the ten worst films of 2011.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

[Todd McCarthy](/wiki/Todd_McCarthy "Todd McCarthy") of *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* gave it a positive review, saying the film "serves up all the requisite elements with enough self-deprecating humor to suggest it doesn't take itself too seriously".[[111]](#cite_note-112) Reviewer [Leonard Maltin](/wiki/Leonard_Maltin "Leonard Maltin") felt that "the film offers a dazzling array of visual effects, a likable hero, a beautiful leading lady, a colorful villain, and a good backstory. It also doesn't take itself too seriously".[[112]](#cite_note-113) [Kenneth Turan](/wiki/Kenneth_Turan "Kenneth Turan") of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* wrote: "More science-fiction space opera than superhero epic, it works in fits and starts as its disparate parts go in and out of effectiveness, but the professionalism of the production make it watchable in a comic book kind of way".[[113]](#cite_note-114)

### Response from cast and crew

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Response from cast and crew")]

After he watched *Green Lantern* for the first time in March 2021, Reynolds tweeted, "Maybe it's the [Aviation Gin](/wiki/Aviation_Gin "Aviation Gin") talking, but #GreenLantern was nothing to fear! Hundreds of incredible crew and cast members did amazing work—and while it's not perfect, it ain't a tragedy. Next time I won't wait a decade to watch."[[114]](#cite_note-115)

In August 2021, director Martin Campbell said, "The film did not work, really. That's the point, and I'm partly responsible for that. I shouldn't have done it. Because with something like [Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") - I love Bond, and I watched every Bond film before I ever directed it. Superhero movies are not my cup of tea, and for that reason, I shouldn't have done it."[[115]](#cite_note-116)

in December 2024 while promoting *[September 5](/wiki/September_5_(film) "September 5 (film)")*, Sarsgaard later said while the film wasn't good, he enjoyed the experience on set including when he killed off [Tim Robbins](/wiki/Tim_Robbins "Tim Robbins")'s character.[[116]](#cite_note-117)

## Franchise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Franchise")]

### Cancelled sequel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Cancelled sequel")]

In 2010, director Martin Campbell confirmed the possibility of a *Green Lantern* trilogy. [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures") originally planned on *Green Lantern* being the first entry of a new DC film series, and commissioned a script for a [sequel](/wiki/Sequel "Sequel") from Greg Berlanti, Michael Green, and Marc Guggenheim while filming for the first film was underway.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

In August 2010, Michael Goldenberg was still attached to write the screenplay, based on the sequel treatment.[[124]](#cite_note-126) The scene in the film's end credits showing a resurgence of the [yellow power ring of fear](/wiki/Sinestro_Corps "Sinestro Corps"), with [Sinestro](/wiki/Sinestro "Sinestro") becoming corrupted by its dark power, teased the planned sequel.[[125]](#cite_note-HReporterSequel-127)

In September 2011, Warner Bros., dismayed by the film's negative reviews and disappointing box office run, abandoned plans for sequels.[[126]](#cite_note-128)

### Cancelled DC Extended Universe reboot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Cancelled DC Extended Universe reboot")]

Main article: [DC Extended Universe](/wiki/DC_Extended_Universe "DC Extended Universe")

In June 2013, [David S. Goyer](/wiki/David_S._Goyer "David S. Goyer") confirmed that *[Man of Steel](/wiki/Man_of_Steel_(film) "Man of Steel (film)")* would be the first film in the [DC Extended Universe](/wiki/DC_Extended_Universe "DC Extended Universe"), implying that should the Green Lantern property be featured in an upcoming DC film, it would be a rebooted version.[[127]](#cite_note-129) Later that year Goyer stated that he had interest in making a new feature film based on the titular character.[[128]](#cite_note-130) By July 2015, Warner Bros. announced plans to release a solo Green Lantern film titled *Green Lantern Corps*, with an original release date scheduled for June 19, 2020. The film was to be an installment in the DC Extended Universe, with Hal Jordan and [John Stewart](/wiki/John_Stewart_(character) "John Stewart (character)") as Green Lanterns in the film.[[129]](#cite_note-131)[[130]](#cite_note-132) In January 2016, Goyer and Justin Rhodes were attached to write the film's script and to produce the film, with [Geoff Johns](/wiki/Geoff_Johns "Geoff Johns") and Jon Berg as executive producers.[[131]](#cite_note-133) As of November 2019, delivery of the script to Warner Bros. was anticipated for the end of that year.[[132]](#cite_note-134) In March 2021, after the release of *[Zack Snyder's Justice League](/wiki/Zack_Snyder%27s_Justice_League "Zack Snyder's Justice League")*, the [director's cut](/wiki/Director%27s_cut "Director's cut") of *[Justice League](/wiki/Justice_League_(film) "Justice League (film)")*, [Zack Snyder](/wiki/Zack_Snyder "Zack Snyder") revealed that a scrapped idea for the film had Batman meeting up with the Green Lantern Corps, with Ryan Reynolds appearing as an "additional lantern... to fill out the corps a bit". Snyder never spoke with Reynolds about this, however.[[133]](#cite_note-135)

### Cancelled Greg Berlanti television series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Cancelled Greg Berlanti television series")]

A 10 one-hour episodic Green Lantern television series separate from the film was in development for [HBO Max](/wiki/HBO_Max "HBO Max") from [Marc Guggenheim](/wiki/Marc_Guggenheim "Marc Guggenheim") and [Seth Grahame-Smith](/wiki/Seth_Grahame-Smith "Seth Grahame-Smith") with Berlanti Productions producing the series. The series was to focus on the [Guy Gardner](/wiki/Guy_Gardner_(character) "Guy Gardner (character)"), [Jessica Cruz](/wiki/Jessica_Cruz "Jessica Cruz"), [Simon Baz](/wiki/Simon_Baz "Simon Baz"), and [Alan Scott](/wiki/Alan_Scott "Alan Scott") versions of Green Lantern alongside Sinestro and Kilowog.[[134]](#cite_note-136) The version with Berlanti was confirmed to have been cancelled, with this new series focusing on Hal Jordan and John Stewart as part of [DC Studios](/wiki/DC_Studios "DC Studios")' new shared universe.[[135]](#cite_note-137)

### DC Universe reboot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: DC Universe reboot")]

Main article: [DC Universe (franchise)](/wiki/DC_Universe_(franchise) "DC Universe (franchise)")

In December 2022, [DC Studios](/wiki/DC_Studios "DC Studios") CEO [James Gunn](/wiki/James_Gunn "James Gunn") confirmed that the Green Lantern characters would be an important part of the new [DCU](/wiki/DC_Universe_(franchise) "DC Universe (franchise)").[[136]](#cite_note-GunnDec2022Important-138) When he and co-CEO [Peter Safran](/wiki/Peter_Safran "Peter Safran") unveiled the first projects from their DCU slate later that month they included *[Lanterns](/wiki/Lanterns_(TV_series) "Lanterns (TV series)")*, a new iteration of a long-in-development Green Lantern series. Safran said the series would be an Earth-based [detective story](/wiki/Detective_fiction "Detective fiction") and "a huge [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO")-quality event" in the style of the series *[True Detective](/wiki/True_Detective "True Detective")* featuring Hal Jordan and John Stewart.[[137]](#cite_note-DCUSlateVariety-139) Safran said the mystery that Jordan and Stewart investigate in the series leads into the main storyline for the DCU.[[137]](#cite_note-DCUSlateVariety-139) In October 2024, [Kyle Chandler](/wiki/Kyle_Chandler "Kyle Chandler") and [Aaron Pierre](/wiki/Aaron_Pierre_(actor) "Aaron Pierre (actor)") were cast as Jordan and Stewart respectively.[[138]](#cite_note-140)[[139]](#cite_note-141)

In July 2023, [Nathan Fillion](/wiki/Nathan_Fillion "Nathan Fillion") was cast as [Guy Gardner](/wiki/Guy_Gardner_(character) "Guy Gardner (character)") in *[Superman](/wiki/Superman_(2025_film) "Superman (2025 film)")*. He is also set to reprise the role in *Lanterns*.[[140]](#cite_note-142)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Lantern_(film)&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The 2016 film *[Deadpool](/wiki/Deadpool_(film) "Deadpool (film)")*, which stars Reynolds as the titular character, refers to the film in two scenes: the opening scene, which features a drawing of Green Lantern, and a later scene where [Deadpool](/wiki/Wade_Wilson_(film_character) "Wade Wilson (film character)") (who breaks the [fourth wall](/wiki/Fourth_wall "Fourth wall")) insists that his suit must not be "green or animated".[[141]](#cite_note-143) In *[Deadpool 2](/wiki/Deadpool_2 "Deadpool 2")*, there is a [post-credits scene](/wiki/Post-credits_scene "Post-credits scene") in which Deadpool travels back in time to kill Reynolds before he can make the film.[[142]](#cite_note-144) Before that, in a 2018 advertising campaign for *Deadpool 2* which also featured [David Beckham](/wiki/David_Beckham "David Beckham"), Reynolds (dressed as Deadpool) is seen apologizing for his critical and commercial flops, including *Green Lantern*, *[R.I.P.D.](/wiki/R.I.P.D. "R.I.P.D.")*, *[Self/less](/wiki/Self/less "Self/less")*, and *[Blade: Trinity](/wiki/Blade:_Trinity "Blade: Trinity")*.[[143]](#cite_note-145)

The DC animated film *[Teen Titans Go! To the Movies](/wiki/Teen_Titans_Go!_To_the_Movies "Teen Titans Go! To the Movies")* (2018) also makes a reference to Green Lantern's disappointing film.[[144]](#cite_note-146)

In a promotional video for *[Free Guy](/wiki/Free_Guy "Free Guy")* in 2019, Reynolds and Waititi, who costarred in both movies, jokingly denied that they had ever heard of *Green Lantern*.[[145]](#cite_note-147)

In [*Sonic the Hedgehog 3*](/wiki/Sonic_the_Hedgehog_3_(film) "Sonic the Hedgehog 3 (film)") (2024), a reference is made to the film when the character of [Dr. Ivo "Eggman" Robotnik](/wiki/Doctor_Eggman "Doctor Eggman"), played by [Jim Carrey](/wiki/Jim_Carrey "Jim Carrey"), jokes to his grandfather Gerald, also played by Carrey, that he hadn't seen a nano-fist since [hate-watching](/wiki/Hate-watching "Hate-watching") it in 2011.[[146]](#cite_note-148)

## Note
